query: when does hypotension occur in hemorrhagic shock according to current medical guidelines

retrieved_docs.title: Hypovolemia and Hypovolemic Shock -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The MAP should be individualized based on the patient's age, history of hypertension, and presence of additional injuries. Recent evidence suggests that the goal of early fluid resuscitation should be to achieve minimally tolerable organ perfusion and continue only to a MAP of 50 to 60 mm Hg. This "permissive hypotension" appears to be beneficial to patients who experience hemorrhagic shock due to torso injuries from gunshot or stab wounds. However, such hypotension is harmful to patients with spinal cord injuries and traumatic brain injuries. European guidelines recommend maintaining a MAP of 50 to 60 mm Hg until active hemorrhage stops. [24] Clinicians should use caution in older patients and those with a history of hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhagic Shock -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hypotensive resuscitation has been suggested for the hemorrhagic shock patient without head trauma. The aim is to achieve a systolic blood pressure of 90 mmHg in order to maintain tissue perfusion without inducing re-bleeding from recently clotted vessels. Permissive hypotension is a means of restricting fluid administration until hemorrhage is controlled while accepting a short period of suboptimal end-organ perfusion. Studies regarding permissive hypotension have yielded conflicting results and must take into account the type of injury (penetrating versus blunt), the likelihood of intracranial injury, the severity of the injury, as well as proximity to a trauma center, and definitive hemorrhage control.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhagic Shock -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Damage control resuscitation is based on three principles: permissive hypotension, hemostatic resuscitation, and damage control surgery.  Permissive hypotension targets a systolic blood pressure of 90 mmHg, accepting suboptimal perfusion to end organs for a limited time to achieve hemostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: Permissive Hypotension -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Hypotensive resuscitation may be used to rapidly stabilize people with hypovolemic shock, particularly in traumatic hemorrhage cases, before definitive hemorrhage control procedures can be performed. However, this intervention is only a temporizing measure. Promptly identifying and addressing the bleeding source are still crucial to patient outcomes. Permissive hypotension should not be employed after controlling bleeding in the operating room.

retrieved_docs.title: Permissive Hypotension -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Incremental fluid infusions are recommended to raise the MAP above 80 mm Hg in cases of significant cerebral hemorrhagic trauma leading to increased intracranial pressure. This intervention allows the maintenance of cerebral perfusion pressure at 60 mm Hg and avoids a potential ischemic event. [11] Recently, recommendations have emerged advocating for the management of SBP based on patient age among patients with TBI. As part of this approach, new thresholds for permissive hypotension were established following a retrospective data review. The Brain Trauma Foundation has set the age-based guidelines well above the permissive hypotension limits: 110 mm Hg in individuals 15 to 49 years of age 100 mm Hg in people 50 to 69 years of age 110 mm Hg in patients 70 years and older [12] [13] Permissive hypotension may be contraindicated in older patients with chronic arterial hypertension. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Permissive Hypotension -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Blood pressure targets are based on the 6th edition of the European guidelines on managing major bleeding and coagulopathy following trauma. For patients with trauma without clinical signs of brain injury, a restricted volume replacement strategy targeting an SBP of 80 to 90 mm Hg or MAP of 50 to 60 mm Hg is recommended until major bleeding is controlled. However, for patients with severe TBI (GCS ≤ 8), a MAP of at least 80 mm Hg is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhagic Shock -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the definition of shock. Summarize the evaluation of hemorrhagic shock. Outline management options available for the treatment of hemorrhagic shock. Identify some interprofessional team strategies for improving care and communication to improve patient outcomes in patients with hemorrhagic shock. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluid Resuscitation -- Indications -- Trauma
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to remember that it is only safe to allow low blood pressures when there is good clinical evidence of adequate organ perfusion indicated through adequate urine output and mental status. [4] Crystalloids should ideally serve as a bridge to maintain perfusion until blood products are available in hemorrhagic shock. Hence, one should consider limited 500-ml bolus doses in patients without or impending shock until blood products become available. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Shock Resuscitation -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: When defining hemorrhagic shock, it is categorized based on the severity of impact on systemic circulation. The corresponding class of shock then determines appropriate intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Permissive Hypotension -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The practice of hypotensive resuscitation has mainly been studied in hemodynamically unstable patients, particularly individuals with severe traumatic hemorrhage. However, the intervention's outcomes in blunt trauma remain unclear. Patients should be evaluated for any prior history that may put them at risk during this procedure. Individuals without a history of chronic hypertension are appropriate candidates for this practice. [7] This technique can worsen preexisting cardiovascular conditions and endocrine abnormalities involving fluid balance, such as syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone and diabetes insipidus.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypovolemia and Hypovolemic Shock -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify early clinical and laboratory signs of volume depletion and systemic hypoperfusion. Differentiate hypovolemic shock from other types of shock to guide appropriate treatment. Implement timely and evidence-based fluid resuscitation protocols tailored to the cause and severity of hypovolemia. Collaborate with interprofessional team members to develop and implement comprehensive, patient-centered treatment plans. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Tactical Damage Control Resuscitation Protocol -- Issues of Concern -- Fluid Resuscitation
retrieved_docs.content: In patients presenting with hypotension and signs suggestive of Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI), especially in the context of multiple injuries and potential internal hemorrhage, hypotensive resuscitation strategies must be tailored to the specific clinical situation. The Western Trauma Association recommends an SBP target of 100 mm Hg for potential TBI cases. In contrast, the Brain Trauma Foundation's 3rd Edition Prehospital Guidelines suggest an SBP above 110 mm Hg in TBI patients, acknowledging a linear relationship between blood pressure and mortality, with higher blood pressure correlating with decreased mortality. They also recognize the need to customize fluid resuscitation strategies in polytrauma patients with TBI and concurrent hemorrhagic shock. Nevertheless, in-hospital guidelines aim for higher blood pressure targets in TBI patients requiring DCR. [33] [34] [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Permissive Hypotension -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Permissive hypotension may worsen outcomes in blunt trauma cases as the intervention may lead to tissue hypoperfusion. Data do not support hypotensive resuscitation's appropriateness in the prehospital stabilization of patients with blunt trauma. On the contrary, high mortality rates have been reported in individuals with blunt trauma in whom this strategy has been attempted.

retrieved_docs.title: Permissive Hypotension -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Moderate crystalloid 0.9% saline amounts have demonstrated some procoagulatory effects. [29] However, caution is advised with certain solutions during resuscitation. Administering colloid dextrans or hydroxyethyl starch solutions may impair coagulation, particularly in quantities exceeding 1.5 liters. [30] Colloids may be considered for patients unable to tolerate large crystalloid volumes, especially when concerns about fluid overload exist. [31] Notably, albumin is contraindicated in patients with TBI.

retrieved_docs.title: Fluid Management -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Few absolute contraindications exist for fluid administration; however, fluid management requires careful consideration of several precautions. [17] Clinicians should generally avoid hypotonic solutions in patients with hyponatremia due to the risk of cerebral edema. Emerging research highlights the dangers of aggressive fluid resuscitation in patients who experience trauma, supporting the concept of "controlled hypotension" or "permissive hypotension." In this approach, fluid administration is limited to maintain a systolic blood pressure just above 70 mm Hg, thereby helping to avoid dilution of clotting factors, disruption of thrombus formation, and worsening of hemorrhage. [18] [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Shock Resuscitation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Roughly 21 million blood products are transfused every year in the United States. Daily, almost 36000 packed RBCs, 7000 platelets, and 10000 FFP units are transfused in the United States. However, the vast majority of these transfusions are not due to hemorrhagic shock, but to prepare a patient for elective surgery (55%), or for patients with some form of chronic anemia (30%). Only 15% of all transfusions in the United States are initiated due to an emergent need for blood products or trauma. Despite the relative infrequency of hemorrhagic shock, it is important to understand the current guidelines regarding appropriate fluid resuscitation in this situation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate is identified as a potential therapeutic agent for the management of shock. Shock, a life‑threatening circulatory failure, is classified in medical terminology as “صدمة إنتانيّة” (infectious shock) and also as “صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ” (cardiogenic shock), reflecting its distinct etiologic subtypes. Recognizing that cortisol succinate may be used to treat these severe shock states underscores its relevance in acute care settings where rapid reversal of hemodynamic collapse is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 306
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone acetate, a corticosteroid formulation, may be employed in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). By providing anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, dexamethasone acetate can help modulate the excessive systemic inflammatory response that characterizes septic shock, potentially improving hemodynamic stability and patient outcomes when used according to appropriate clinical guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2133
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylefrin, a potent α‑adrenergic agonist commonly used as a vasopressor, may be employed in the treatment of صدمة إنتانيّة (septic shock). By inducing peripheral vasoconstriction, fenylefrin can help raise systemic vascular resistance and improve arterial blood pressure in patients whose circulatory collapse is driven by overwhelming infection. Its use in septic shock is typically considered when rapid restoration of hemodynamic stability is required, often in conjunction with fluid resuscitation and other supportive measures. Proper dosing and monitoring are essential to balance the benefits of increased perfusion pressure against the risk of excessive vasoconstriction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1294
retrieved_docs.content: Metaraminol and cortisol succinate are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for the management of shock, indicating that each may be used to treat this life‑threatening condition. Their inclusion as possible treatments highlights the importance of having multiple pharmacologic options when addressing the hemodynamic instability characteristic of shock, thereby supporting tailored patient care and improving the likelihood of stabilizing circulatory function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: (التخثر الوريدي) is identified as a subtype of venous disorder (اضطراب الوريد). This integrated knowledge connects drug classes, contraindications, and therapeutic strategies for shock with the pathophysiology of arterial and venous thromboembolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1365
retrieved_docs.content: Terazosin is an α₁‑adrenergic antagonist that produces arterial vasodilation, a physiologic effect that reduces peripheral vascular resistance and lowers systemic blood pressure. Because of this vasodilatory action, terazosin is contraindicated in patients with ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), as further reduction in vascular tone could precipitate symptomatic hypotension. Although one source incorrectly lists terazosin as having “Endocrine drugs” as a parent category, terazosin is more appropriately classified as a cardiovascular medication rather than an endocrine drug. Recognizing terazosin’s arterial vasodilation effect and its contraindication in hypotensive states is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), indicating that this form of hemorrhagic brain trauma often co‑occurs with internal injuries and should be evaluated together in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1856
retrieved_docs.content: Diphenhydramine hydrochloride is contraindicated in patients with حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (an edematous condition). In contrast, hydrocortisone sodium succinate may be used to treat حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة and can also be employed in the management of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse). Recognizing that diphenhydramine hydrochloride should be avoided in edema while hydrocortisone sodium succinate is appropriate for both edema and severe circulatory failure guides therapeutic decisions and enhances patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2584
retrieved_docs.content: Sildenafil and tadalafil are phosphodiesterase‑5 inhibitor medications that are contraindicated for use with nitroglycerin, a nitrate vasodilator; concurrent administration of either sildenafil or tadalafil with nitroglycerin can lead to severe hypotension and should be avoided in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 412
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب الكلية (kidney disorder), indicating that its use should be avoided in individuals with impaired renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2786
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate, a synthetic glucocorticoid preparation, may be employed in the treatment of shock, where its rapid anti‑inflammatory and vasopressor effects can help stabilize hemodynamics and support organ perfusion in critically ill patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2246
retrieved_docs.content: Tromethamin is categorized within the therapeutic class of Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders, indicating that it functions as a volume‑expanding agent used to restore or maintain intravascular fluid volume. This classification reflects its role in clinical situations such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, or other conditions requiring rapid hemodynamic support, where augmenting plasma volume is essential. Recognizing tromethamin as part of the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders class helps clinicians select appropriate volume‑expanding therapies and understand its mechanism of modifying blood components to increase circulatory volume.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى (renal failure), meaning its use should be avoided in individuals suffering from kidney failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 311
retrieved_docs.content: Shock, a clinical syndrome of inadequate tissue perfusion, is classified as صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ, which denotes cardiogenic shock—a form of shock that originates from primary cardiac dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1601
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is a rapid‑acting glucocorticoid formulation that can be employed in the management of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), providing anti‑inflammatory and hemodynamic support to help stabilize patients experiencing severe cardiovascular failure.
